items.title: [BUG] [alpha] Quick Access Extensions Refresh Extensions closes the picker instead of refreshing results in place
items.body:
### Project

ide

### Description

In **Quick Access -> Extensions**, the visible action row **Refresh Extensions** behaves like an in-place list-management action.

But selecting it does not refresh the currently open picker results.

Visible result:

- open **Quick Access -> Extensions**
- select **Refresh Extensions**
- the picker closes immediately
- the refreshed extension list is not shown in the same Quick Access session

So the field exposes a refresh action inside the picker, but using it exits the picker instead of refreshing the visible results in place.

### Root Cause

The mounted `ext ` provider defines **Refresh Extensions** as an action row:

- `/src/context/QuickAccessContext.tsx:1091-1097`
  - label: **Refresh Extensions**
  - description: **Reload extension list**

Its provider action does call `extensions.loadExtensions()`:

- `/src/context/QuickAccessContext.tsx:1124-1125`

But the generic Quick Access accept path always closes the picker immediately after invoking any provider action:

- `/src/context/QuickAccessContext.tsx:1258-1263`
  - calls `active.provider.onAccept(item)`
  - then always calls `hide()`

So the refresh action cannot update the currently visible picker contents because the picker is already gone.


### Error Message

```shell

```

### Debug Logs

```shell

```

### System Information

```shell
- Cortex IDE `alpha`
- Platform: any
```

### Screenshots

https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/e505b039-88ba-42e9-bbd9-f48daa6b330c

### Steps to Reproduce

1. Open **Quick Access**.
2. Enter **Extensions** mode.
3. Select **Refresh Extensions**.
4. Observe what happens to the picker.


### Expected Behavior

Because **Refresh Extensions** is an in-picker management action, selecting it should refresh the current extension results in place or otherwise keep the user in the field while the refresh completes.


### Actual Behavior

Selecting **Refresh Extensions** closes Quick Access immediately.

The refreshed list is not shown in the same picker session.


### Additional Context

This is not the same as the current live Quick Access Extensions issues:

- `#33291` **Quick Access Extensions search ignores visible Enabled or Disabled status text**
- `#33403` **Quick Access Extensions search ignores visible action labels like Install Extension and Refresh Extensions**
- `#33414` **Quick Access Extensions pin button has no effect on result ordering or recall**
- `#33417` **Quick Access Extensions failed enable or disable closes the picker with no visible error**
- `#33422` **Quick Access Extensions selecting an extension row toggles enable or disable instead of showing extension details**
- `#33438` **Quick Access Extensions has no uninstall action for installed extensions**
- `#33444` **Quick Access Extensions does not remember recent selections**
- `#33451` **Quick Access Extensions Install Extension action opens the installed extensions tab instead of marketplace**
- `#33460` **Quick Access Extensions can dead-end in No results found for not-installed extension names**
- `#33472` **Quick Access Extensions can reopen with stale Enabled or Disabled state while a toggle is still in flight**

Those reports cover search, pinning, failure handling, row-action semantics, uninstall availability, history recall, wrong install destination, dead-end search flow, and stale reopen state.

This report is about the separate **Refresh Extensions** action being unable to refresh the currently open picker in place.

### Duplicate Check

Re-checked local (`bug-3`, `bugs-codex`) and targeted live repo searches with:

- `Quick Access Extensions Refresh Extensions closes`
- `Quick Access Extensions refresh closes picker`
- `Quick Access Extensions refresh in place`
- `Quick Access Extensions reload extension list closes`
- local grep for the **Refresh Extensions** row and the generic `handleAccept(...); hide()` path in `/src/context/QuickAccessContext.tsx`

Findings:

- Local: no matching report found for this specific **Quick Access Extensions Refresh Extensions closes the picker instead of refreshing results in place** path.
- Live repo: no matching issue found in `PlatformNetwork/bounty-challenge` for this exact `Quick Access -> Extensions` in-place refresh bug at check time (`2026-03-19 UTC`).
- Closest live issues are different:
  - `#33417` `Quick Access Extensions failed enable or disable closes the picker with no visible error`
  - `#33451` `Quick Access Extensions Install Extension action opens the installed extensions tab instead of marketplace`
- Why they are different:
  - `#33417` is about failed row toggles and missing error feedback,
  - `#33451` is about the install action opening the wrong destination,
  - this report is about **Refresh Extensions** closing the picker instead of refreshing the visible list in place.

items.title: [BUG] [alpha] AgentActivityFeed FILTER_OPTIONS missing tool_error — users cannot filter for tool error actions
items.body: ### Project ide ### Description The AgentActivityFeed filter dropdown (FILTER_OPTIONS at lines 93-104) lists 9 specific action type filters but is missing `"tool_error"`. The `AgentActionType` union type (ActionEntry.tsx lines 9-19) includes 10 values — `"tool_error"` is the only one without a corresponding filter option. Users cannot isolate tool error actions from the activity feed. **Root cause (lines 93-104 vs ActionEntry.tsx lines 9-19):** FILTER_OPTIONS has entries for: `file_read`, `file_edit`, `file_create`, `file_delete`, `terminal_command`, `terminal_output`, `thinking`, `tool_start`, `tool_complete` — but NOT `tool_error`. AgentActionType includes all 10: the 9 above PLUS `tool_error`. ### Error Message ```shell N/A — missing UI option ``` ### Debug Logs ```shell # ActionEntry.tsx lines 9-19 — AgentActionType includes tool_error: export type AgentActionType = | "file_read" | "file_edit" | "file_create" | "file_delete" | "terminal_command" | "terminal_output" | "thinking" | "tool_start" | "tool_complete" | "tool_error"; // <-- this type exists # AgentActivityFeed.tsx lines 93-104 — FILTER_OPTIONS missing tool_error: const FILTER_OPTIONS: { value: FilterType; label: string; icon: string }[] = [ { value: "all", label: "All Actions", icon: "wave-pulse" }, { value: "file_read", label: "File Reads", icon: "file" }, { value: "file_edit", label: "File Edits", icon: "pen" }, { value: "file_create", label: "File Creates", icon: "file" }, { value: "file_delete", label: "File Deletes", icon: "file" }, { value: "terminal_command", label: "Commands", icon: "terminal" }, { value: "terminal_output", label: "Output", icon: "terminal" }, { value: "thinking", label: "Thinking", icon: "microchip" }, { value: "tool_start", label: "Tool Start", icon: "wrench" }, { value: "tool_complete", label: "Tool Complete", icon: "wrench" }, // MISSING: { value: "tool_error", label: "Tool Errors", icon: "circle-exclamation" } ]; ``` ### System Information ```shell Cortex IDE: alpha OS: Microsoft Windows 11 Home Single Language Component: src/components/ai/AgentActivityFeed.tsx, lines 93-104 (FILTER_OPTIONS) ``` ### Screenshots ### Steps to Reproduce 1. Open Cortex IDE and start an AI agent session. 2. Trigger some actions including tool calls that fail (producing tool_error entries). 3. Open the Activity Feed panel in the AI Agents sidebar. 4. Click the filter dropdown to filter by action type. 5. Observe that the dropdown has filters for "Tool Start" and "Tool Complete" but no "Tool Errors" option. 6. Tool error actions can only be seen in the "All Actions" view — they cannot be isolated. ### Expected Behavior The filter dropdown should include a "Tool Errors" option (matching the `"tool_error"` value in `AgentActionType`) so users can filter the feed for only error actions, similar to how "Tool Start" and "Tool Complete" are available. ### Actual Behavior The `FILTER_OPTIONS` array at lines 93-104 has entries for 9 of the 10 `AgentActionType` values but omits `"tool_error"`. Users cannot filter the activity feed for tool error actions.

items.title: [BUG] [alpha] Quick Access Extensions can dead-end in No results found for not-installed extension names
items.body:
### Project

ide

### Description

In **Quick Access -> Extensions**, typing the name of an extension that is not currently installed can lead to a dead end.

Visible result:

- open **Quick Access -> Extensions**
- type the name of an extension that is not installed
- no installed extension rows match
- **Install Extension...** is not offered as a fallback
- the picker shows **No results found**

So the field can present a blank dead end even though it already has an install action and the natural next step would be to use it.

### Root Cause

The mounted **Extensions** provider filters only the installed extension list against the query:

- `/src/context/QuickAccessContext.tsx:1048-1053`
  - only installed extension names and descriptions are checked

The install action is not kept as a general fallback when there are no matches.

Instead it is shown only for empty query or a few hardcoded keywords:

- `/src/context/QuickAccessContext.tsx:1075-1081`
  - install action depends on:
  - `!trimmedQuery`
  - `"install".includes(trimmedQuery)`
  - `"marketplace".includes(trimmedQuery)`

If the query is a not-installed extension name like `prettier`, none of those conditions are true.

So the picker falls through to the generic empty state:

- `/src/context/QuickAccessContext.tsx:1504-1510`
  - shows **No results found**


### Error Message

```shell

```

### Debug Logs

```shell

```

### System Information

```shell
- Cortex IDE `alpha`
- Platform: any
```

### Screenshots

https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/a1f1eb73-7e0f-4931-a9c9-072cce187eb6

### Steps to Reproduce

1. Open **Quick Access**.
2. Enter **Extensions** mode.
3. Type the name of an extension that is not installed.
4. Observe the results list.


### Expected Behavior

If no installed extension matches the query, the field should still offer **Install Extension...** or another marketplace/install path instead of dead-ending.


### Actual Behavior

The mounted picker can show **No results found** for a not-installed extension name even though the field has an install action.

The install path disappears unless the query happens to match a small hardcoded keyword set.


### Additional Context

This is not the same as the current live Quick Access Extensions issues:

- `#33291` **Quick Access Extensions search ignores visible Enabled or Disabled status text**
- `#33403` **Quick Access Extensions search ignores visible action labels like Install Extension and Refresh Extensions**
- `#33414` **Quick Access Extensions pin button has no effect on result ordering or recall**
- `#33417` **Quick Access Extensions failed enable or disable closes the picker with no visible error**
- `#33422` **Quick Access Extensions selecting an extension row toggles enable or disable instead of showing extension details**
- `#33438` **Quick Access Extensions has no uninstall action for installed extensions**
- `#33444` **Quick Access Extensions does not remember recent selections**

Those reports cover visible-text search mismatches, pinning, failure handling, row action behavior, uninstall availability, and recent-history recall.

This report is about the separate no-match dead end when the query is a not-installed extension name.

### Duplicate Check

Re-checked local (`bug-3`, `bugs-codex`) and targeted live repo searches with:

- `Quick Access Extensions No results found`
- `Quick Access Extensions not installed`
- `Quick Access Extensions install action missing on no match`
- `Quick Access Extensions dead end`
- local grep for installed-extension filtering, install-action gating, and the generic empty-state fallback in `/src/context/QuickAccessContext.tsx`

Findings:

- Local: no matching report found for this specific **Quick Access Extensions can dead-end in No results found for not-installed extension names** path.
- Live repo: no matching issue found in `PlatformNetwork/bounty-challenge` for this exact `Quick Access -> Extensions` no-match dead-end bug at check time (`2026-03-19 UTC`).
- Closest live issues are different:
  - `#33291` `Quick Access Extensions search ignores visible Enabled or Disabled status text`
  - `#33403` `Quick Access Extensions search ignores visible action labels like Install Extension and Refresh Extensions`
- Why they are different:
  - those reports are about searching visible text already present in the picker,
  - this report is about the mounted picker falling into **No results found** instead of still offering an install path for a not-installed extension name.

items.title: Ubounty Bounty Integration
items.body:
### 🎯 Goal Allow maintainers to create Ubounty bounties directly from Ascencia ### ✅ Acceptance Criteria - [ ] "Fund via Ubounty" button on issue creation - [ ] Link opens Ubounty with pre-filled issue URL - [ ] Badge shows "Guaranteed by Ubounty" - [ ] Payment method stored in database - [ ] Documentation explains both payment methods ### 📝 Tasks #### 1. Update Database Schema (15 min) Add to existing `issues` table: ```sql -- Run in Supabase SQL Editor ALTER TABLE issues ADD COLUMN payment_method TEXT CHECK (payment_method IN ('direct_tip', 'ubounty', 'x402')); ALTER TABLE issues ADD COLUMN ubounty_bounty_id TEXT; ALTER TABLE issues ADD COLUMN ubounty_url TEXT; ``` #### 2. Create Ubounty Helper (30 min) Create `base-oss-app-chain/lib/ubounty.ts`: ```typescript export interface UbountyBountyParams { githubIssueUrl: string amount: number // in USDC network?: 'base-sepolia' | 'base-mainnet' } export function createUbountyUrl(params: UbountyBountyParams): string { const baseUrl = 'https://ubounty.ai/create-bounty' const searchParams = new URLSearchParams({ issue_url: params.githubIssueUrl, amount: params.amount.toString(), network: params.network || 'base-sepolia', // Add UTM parameters for tracking utm_source: 'ascencia', utm_medium: 'platform', }) return `${baseUrl}?${searchParams.toString()}` } export function parseUbountyWebhook(payload: any) { // Parse Ubounty webhook when bounty is paid return { bountyId: payload.bounty_id, issueUrl: payload.issue_url, amount: payload.amount_usdc, contributor: payload.developer_address, txHash: payload.payment_tx_hash, paidAt: payload.paid_at, } } ``` #### 3. Update Issue Creation Form (45 min) Create `components/issues/CreateIssueForm.tsx`: ```typescript 'use client' import { useState } from 'react' import { supabase } from '@/lib/supabase' import { createUbountyUrl } from '@/lib/ubounty' export function CreateIssueForm() { const [paymentMethod, setPaymentMethod] = useState<'direct_tip' | 'ubounty'>('direct_tip') const [githubUrl, setGithubUrl] = useState('') const [bountyAmount, setBountyAmount] = useState(50) const handleSubmit = async (e: React.FormEvent) => { e.preventDefault() if (paymentMethod === 'ubounty') { // Redirect to Ubounty const ubountyUrl = createUbountyUrl({ githubIssueUrl: githubUrl, amount: bountyAmount, network: 'base-sepolia', }) window.open(ubountyUrl, '_blank') // Store that this issue uses Ubounty await supabase .from('issues') .update({ payment_method: 'ubounty', ubounty_url: ubountyUrl, }) .eq('github_issue_url', githubUrl) } else { // Direct tip flow (existing) } } return (
setGithubUrl(e.target.value)} placeholder="https://github.com/org/repo/issues/123" className="w-full px-4 py-2 border rounded-lg" required />
{/* Direct Tip */} {/* Ubounty */}
{paymentMethod === 'ubounty' && (
setBountyAmount(Number(e.target.value))} min={10} max={1000} className="w-full px-4 py-2 border rounded-lg" required />

+ $0.99 Ubounty intent fee

)}
) } ``` #### 4. Add Ubounty Badge to Issue Card (20 min) Update `components/issues/IssueCard.tsx`: ```typescript export function IssueCard({ issue }: IssueCardProps) { const isUbountyBounty = issue.payment_method === 'ubounty' return ( {/* ... existing code ... */} {/* Ubounty Badge */} {isUbountyBounty && (
🔒 Guaranteed by Ubounty {issue.ubounty_url && ( href={issue.ubounty_url} target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer" className="text-xs text-blue-600 hover:underline" onClick={(e) => e.stopPropagation()} > View on Ubounty → )}
)} ) } ``` #### 5. Documentation (20 min) Docs: https://ubounty.ai/bounties Create `docs/PAYMENT_METHODS.md`: ```markdown # Payment Methods in Ascencia Ascencia supports multiple ways to reward contributors: ## 1. Direct Tip (Recommended for Most Issues) **How it works:** - Maintainer creates issue on Ascencia - Contributor completes work - Maintainer tips via x402 after reviewing - XP recorded on Base blockchain **Pros:** - No upfront cost - Flexible amount based on quality - Integrated with Ascencia's XP system **Cons:** - Payment not guaranteed upfront - Requires maintainer action after merge ## 2. Ubounty Bounty (Recommended for High-Value Work) **How it works:** - Maintainer creates bounty on Ubounty.ai - Bounty is pre-funded ($0.99 fee) - Contributor submits PR with `Fixes #123` - Payment automatically released on merge - Ascencia records XP after payment **Pros:** - Guaranteed payment (escrowed) - Automatic release on merge - No maintainer action needed **Cons:** - $0.99 intent fee - Requires upfront funding - External platform dependency ## Integration with Proof of Cycle Both payment methods integrate with Ascencia's Proof of Cycle: - Contribution recorded on-chain - XP awarded based on difficulty - Leaderboard updated - Cycle completion tracked The payment method is just HOW funds flow - the reputation system works the same regardless. ``` ### ✅ Success Criteria - Can click "Fund via Ubounty" and redirect to Ubounty - Ubounty badge displays on issues - Database stores payment method - Documentation explains both methods

items.title: [RFC] Community-Led Growth Strategy: Positioning Hive as the Production-Grade Agent Framework for 2026
items.body:
## Executive Summary

The agentic AI framework market is at an inflection point. Developer adoption is accelerating, enterprise demand is exploding, but most developers are overwhelmed by fragmented options and unclear differentiation. Hive has a genuine technical moat—self-evolving agents that improve from failures—but this advantage isn't yet translating into proportional market share.

This RFC proposes a comprehensive growth strategy designed to:
1. **Establish Hive as the category leader in "self-evolving agents"** — owning a narrative no competitor can claim
2. **Accelerate community growth from 7.5K to 25K stars in 12 months** — through strategic content, partnerships, and developer experience improvements
3. **Build a contributor flywheel** — converting users into advocates into contributors
4. **Position Hive for enterprise adoption** — with production-ready positioning and social proof

I've spent significant time analyzing Hive's architecture, the competitive landscape, and developer sentiment across GitHub, Hacker News, and various AI communities. What follows is a data-driven strategy with specific tactics, timelines, and success metrics.

---

## Part 1: Market Context & Opportunity

### 1.1 The 2026 Agentic AI Landscape

The market has fundamentally shifted. According to industry analysis, the question for developers has moved from "What is an AI Agent?" to "Which framework should I use to build one?" This is the critical window for establishing market position.

**Market Size Trajectory:**

| Year | Market Size | Growth Driver |
|------|-------------|---------------|
| 2024 | $7.8B | Early adoption, experimentation |
| 2025 | $12.4B | Production deployments begin |
| 2026 | $19.2B | Enterprise acceleration |
| 2030 | $52B+ | Mainstream adoption |

Gartner predicts 40% of enterprise applications will embed AI agents by end of 2026, up from less than 5% in 2025. This represents a 700%+ increase in 12 months—the fastest enterprise adoption curve since cloud computing.

### 1.2 Competitive Landscape Analysis

**Current Framework Market Share (by GitHub stars, February 2026):**

| Framework | Stars | Monthly Growth | Primary Positioning |
|-----------|-------|----------------|---------------------|
| AutoGPT | 164K+ | Declining | Autonomous agents (viral, but fading) |
| LangChain | 95K+ | +2.1%/mo | General LLM framework |
| AutoGen | 40K+ | +3.4%/mo | Multi-agent conversations |
| LlamaIndex | 38K+ | +2.8%/mo | RAG and data retrieval |
| CrewAI | 25K+ | +5.2%/mo | Role-based multi-agent |
| LangGraph | 14K+ | +4.1%/mo | Stateful graph workflows |
| **Hive** | **7.5K** | **+6.8%/mo** | **Self-evolving agents** |

**Key Insight:** Hive has the highest percentage growth rate among production-grade frameworks. The community is responding to the self-evolution approach. The opportunity is to accelerate this momentum before well-funded competitors close the gap.

### 1.3 Developer Pain Points (From Community Research)

I analyzed discussions across Hacker News, Reddit, and competitor issue trackers. The top developer frustrations with existing frameworks:

> "The whole landscape seems broken and unproductive. Countless vendors, platforms, cloud environments—all with different pricing models, SLAs, tooling."

> "Forty seconds into the demo, the agent called the same API three times, hallucinated a policy we didn't have, then got stuck in a loop. The framework you choose determines failure modes you won't see until production."

> "LangGraph is a beautifully engineered cage for deterministic thinkers."

**Pain Point Priority Matrix:**

| Pain Point | Severity | Hive's Current Answer | Opportunity |
|------------|----------|----------------------|-------------|
| Agents break in production | Critical | Self-evolution | Needs better messaging |
| Unpredictable costs | Critical | Budget controls | Needs visibility |
| Manual workflow maintenance | High | Dynamic graph generation | Needs examples |
| Debugging complexity | High | Observability features | Needs documentation |
| Integration fragmentation | Medium | MCP-native approach | Needs expansion |

---

## Part 2: Strategic Positioning

### 2.1 The Narrative Gap

Hive's core differentiator—self-evolving agents—is genuinely unique. No other framework automatically captures failure data, evolves the agent graph, and redeploys without human intervention. This is not incremental improvement; it's a paradigm shift.

**But the market doesn't understand this yet.**

Current messaging challenge: "Self-evolving" sounds like marketing jargon. Developers are skeptical. They've heard "AI that improves itself" before and been disappointed.

**Proposed Narrative Framework:**

Instead of leading with "self-evolving," lead with the **problem it solves**:

> **Old narrative:** "Build self-evolving AI agents"
> 
> **New narrative:** "The only framework where your agents get smarter in production—not dumber"

This reframe:
- Acknowledges a known pain (agents degrade over time)
- Positions the differentiator as a solution
- Creates a memorable contrast

### 2.2 Category Creation: "Outcome-Driven Development"

Hive already uses the term "Outcome-Driven Development" (ODD) in documentation. This is an underleveraged asset. The opportunity is to **own a category**, not just compete in an existing one.

**Category Creation Strategy:**

| Element | Current State | Proposed State |
|---------|---------------|----------------|
| Term | Used internally | Industry-recognized methodology |
| Content | Mentioned in docs | Dedicated landing page, blog series, conference talks |
| Education | None | "ODD Certification" or badge program |
| Comparison | Implicit | Explicit "ODD vs. Traditional Agent Development" content |

**Why This Matters:** 

When HubSpot coined "Inbound Marketing," they didn't just describe their product—they created a movement that positioned them as the thought leader. Hive has the same opportunity with Outcome-Driven Development.

---

## Part 3: Growth Engine Design

### 3.1 Content Strategy

**Objective:** Establish Hive as the authoritative voice on production-grade AI agents through consistent, high-value content.

**Content Pillar Structure:**

| Pillar | Topics | Format | Frequency | Primary Channel |
|--------|--------|--------|-----------|-----------------|
| Technical Deep-Dives | Architecture, self-evolution mechanics, MCP integration | Long-form blog (2000+ words) | Bi-weekly | Blog → Dev.to → HN |
| Tutorials | "Build X agent in Y minutes" | Step-by-step guides | Weekly | Docs + YouTube |
| Comparisons | Hive vs. LangGraph, CrewAI, etc. | Honest technical analysis | Monthly | Blog → Reddit |
| Case Studies | Production deployments, metrics | Customer stories | Monthly | Blog → LinkedIn |
| Thought Leadership | Future of agents, ODD methodology | Opinion pieces | Monthly | Medium → Twitter |

**Specific Content Calendar (First 8 Weeks):**

| Week | Content Piece | Target Reach | Distribution |
|------|---------------|--------------|--------------|
| 1 | "Why Your Agents Get Dumber in Production (And How to Fix It)" | 15K views | Dev.to → HN → Twitter |
| 2 | "Build a Self-Improving Customer Support Agent in 20 Minutes" | 8K views | Docs → YouTube |
| 3 | "Hive vs. LangGraph: A Honest Technical Comparison" | 20K views | Blog → Reddit → HN |
| 4 | "The True Cost of Agent Failures: Why Runtime Governance Matters" | 10K views | Medium → LinkedIn |
| 5 | "Outcome-Driven Development: A New Paradigm for AI Agents" | 12K views | Blog → Twitter thread |
| 6 | "From Zero to Production: Deploying Hive Agents on Kubernetes" | 8K views | Docs → Dev.to |
| 7 | "How [Company X] Reduced Agent Maintenance by 73% with Self-Evolution" | 15K views | Blog → LinkedIn |
| 8 | "The Agent Framework Decision Matrix: Which Tool for Which Job" | 25K views | Blog → HN → Reddit |

**Content Quality Bar:**
- Every technical claim backed by code examples
- Honest about limitations (builds credibility)
- Includes reproduction steps
- Optimized for search ("AI agent framework," "LangChain alternative," "production AI agents")

### 3.2 Community Growth Tactics

**Tactic 1: Template Marketplace**

Current state: Users create agents from scratch or follow quickstart.
Proposed state: Library of production-ready templates for common use cases.

| Template Category | Examples | Estimated Demand |
|-------------------|----------|------------------|
| Customer Support | FAQ bot, ticket classifier, escalation agent | Very High |
| Sales Automation | Lead qualifier, outreach personalizer, CRM updater | High |
| Data Processing | ETL agent, data validator, report generator | High |
| DevOps | Incident responder, log analyzer, deployment agent | Medium |
| Content | Writer, editor, social media manager | Medium |

**Implementation:** Create `exports/templates/` directory with 10 production-ready templates in first 90 days. Each template includes:
- Complete agent code
- Configuration options
- Documentation
- Test suite
- Example outputs

**Expected Impact:** Templates reduce time-to-value, increase activation, and provide contribution opportunities for community members.

---

**Tactic 2: Contributor Recognition Program**

Current state: Contributors mentioned in release notes (good start).
Proposed state: Formal recognition program with clear incentives.

**Proposed Tiers:**

| Tier | Criteria | Benefits |
|------|----------|----------|
| 🥉 Contributor | 1 merged PR | Name in release notes, Discord role |
| 🥈 Active Contributor | 3+ merged PRs | Swag pack, early feature access |
| 🥇 Core Contributor | 10+ PRs or significant feature | Direct team access, conference sponsorship |
| 💎 Maintainer | Ongoing significant contribution | Potential hire consideration, equity discussion |

**Why This Matters:** Vincent mentioned "We've hired 4 contributors so far." Making this path visible and formalized encourages more serious contribution.

---

**Tactic 3: "Hive Showcase" Monthly Spotlight**

- Community members submit agents they've built
- Team selects 3-5 for monthly spotlight
- Featured in newsletter, social, and docs
- Builds social proof while celebrating community

---

**Tactic 4: Integration Bounty Program**

Current need: More integrations (per issue #2805).
Proposed solution: Bounty program for priority integrations.

| Integration | Priority | Bounty | Business Case |
|-------------|----------|--------|---------------|
| Salesforce | P0 | $500 | Enterprise CRM automation |
| Linear | P0 | $300 | DevOps workflow integration |
| Notion | P1 | $300 | Knowledge management |
| Slack | P1 | $300 | Team communication |
| HubSpot | P1 | $300 | Marketing automation |
| Jira | P2 | $200 | Enterprise project management |
| Zendesk | P2 | $200 | Customer support |

**Expected Impact:** Accelerate integration coverage while rewarding community and building relationships with potential hires.

### 3.3 Developer Experience Optimization

**North Star Metric:** Time to First Successful Agent < 10 minutes

**Current Friction Points (from issue analysis):**

| Friction Point | Current State | Proposed Solution |
|----------------|---------------|-------------------|
| API key confusion | Unclear if required, how to get | Add sandbox mode, clear docs |
| First-time contributor onboarding | Overwhelming for newcomers | Interactive contributor guide |
| Windows support | Recently added, still rough | Dedicated Windows troubleshooting |
| Local LLM setup | Documented but buried | Prominent Ollama quickstart |

**Quick Wins (< 1 week effort each):**

1. **Interactive Quickstart:** Add `hive init` command that walks through setup interactively
2. **Sandbox Mode:** Allow exploration without API keys using mock responses
3. **Error Message Improvement:** When agents fail, explain why in plain language with suggested fixes
4. **Progress Indicators:** Show clear feedback during agent execution

### 3.4 Strategic Partnerships

### 4.3 Competitive Tracking

**Success Criteria (Month 3):**

---

## Closing Thoughts

Hive has something genuinely special: a technical approach to agent development that addresses the #1 production pain point (agents that break and require constant maintenance). The self-evolution loop isn't a feature—it's a philosophy that aligns with how AI systems should work.

The window for establishing category leadership is now. LangChain has ecosystem dominance but architectural baggage. CrewAI is growing but lacks production sophistication. AutoGen is powerful but unpredictable. Hive can own the "production-grade, self-improving" positioning if it moves decisively.

I wrote this analysis because I believe in what Hive is building. I've tried to be specific and actionable rather than generic and aspirational. Every recommendation here is something I'd be excited to help execute.

items.title: RAGE Streaming Budget (grcResourceCache) Saturates to 100% From Base Game Assets Alone — Build-Independent, Reproducible on Localhost With Zero Custom Content

labels.name: triage
labels.description: Needs a preliminary assessment to determine the urgency and required action

items.body: ### What happened? # RAGE Streaming Budget (grcResourceCache) Saturates to 100% From Base Game Assets Alone — Build-Independent, Reproducible on Localhost With Zero Custom Content ## Summary The RAGE streaming pool (`grcore/ResourceCache`) fills to 99.7–100% capacity from base game assets alone, with zero custom streamed content. This is reproducible on localhost with no server infrastructure involved. The behavior is **not build-dependent** — tested on builds 3095 (The Chop Shop), 3258 (Bottom Dollar Bounties), and 3570 (Money Fronts), all producing the same saturation. The budget saturates on all tested VRAM tiers (10 GB, 12 GB, 16 GB), at any Extended Texture Budget slider setting, and does not release while stationary. On ≤12 GB VRAM cards, this results in synchronous evict-load stalls causing 0.5–2 second client freezes during area transitions. ## Reproduction Steps **Environment — minimal localhost, zero custom content:** - FiveM localhost (no remote server, no server artifact dependency) - Game builds tested: **3095** (The Chop Shop), **3258** (Bottom Dollar Bounties), **3570** (Money Fronts) — all produce the same saturation - OneSync: enabled - Custom streamed assets: **NONE** — Lua script resources only, no maps, no vehicles, no clothing, no MLOs - No `increase_pool_size` overrides relevant to streaming - No streaming-related convars in server.cfg **Steps:** 1. Launch FiveM with `+set moo 31338`, connect to localhost 2. Open F8 console, run `strmem` 3. Observe `grcore/ResourceCache` starting at ~2,200–3,500 MiB after spawn 4. Drive or walk through the base game city — no specific route required, normal traversal through Los Santosa Traversal via noclip accelerates the saturation due to covering more map area in less time, but the same saturation occurs with normal on-foot/vehicle movement — it simply takes longer to reach ceiling. 5. Observe `grcore/ResourceCache` climbing to 99.7–100% of its budget ceiling 6. Stop moving and stand still 7. Observe the budget remaining pinned at ceiling with no meaningful eviction (only ~5 MiB fluctuation from LOD mip swaps) **This has been reproduced on:** | GPU | VRAM | Budget Ceiling (approx) | Saturated After Traversal? | |-----|------|------------------------|---------------------------| | RTX 5080 Laptop | 16 GB | ~9,639 MiB | Yes — 9,607 / 9,639 (99.7%) | | RTX 4070 | 12 GB | ~8,460 MiB | Yes — 8,460 / 8,460 (99.99%) | | RTX 3080 | 10–12 GB | ~7,000–8,000 MiB | Yes (100%) — severe freezes | **Lowering the Extended Texture Budget slider** reduces the ceiling but the pool still fills to 100% of whatever budget is allocated. With a reduced budget on the RTX 5080, usage went from 0 → 3,520 / 3,520 MiB (100%) from the same city traversal. ## Top Streaming Consumers (All Base Game / DLC Assets) From the `strmem` output on the stripped localhost at budget saturation — no custom content present: | Asset | Total (MiB) | Source | |-------|-------------|--------| | cs5_3_cs5_03_pwrst+hi.ytd | 57.02 | Base game city texture | | globalroads+hi.ytd | 57.02 | Base game roads | | core.ypt | 57.14 | Base game particle system | | cs6_06_land.ytd | 49.53 | Base game city texture | | hei_dt1_carrmp.ytd | 38.77 | Cayo Perico Heist DLC | | ch_chint03_dlc_plan.ytd | 37.03 | Casino DLC interior | | platform:/textures/fxdecal.ytd | 33.02 | Base game | | ch_chint01_vwint01_vwdlc_vw_casino.ytd | 23.77 | Casino DLC interior | | ch_chint06_dlc_casino_back.ytd | 21.52 | Casino DLC interior | | ch_chint08_dlc_casino_loading.ytd | 21.01 | Casino DLC interior | | ch_chint07_dlc_casino_hotel.ytd | 20.27 | Casino DLC interior | | ch_chint09_dlc_vault.ytd | 20.27 | Casino DLC interior | | tr_sc1_02_tuner_hd.ydr | 24.07 | Tuner DLC | | m24_1_mp2024_01_additions_carrmp.ytd | 22.02 | Bottom Dollar Bounties DLC | Casino DLC interior textures alone consume approximately 144+ MiB from proximity preloading — without the player entering the interior. ## Key Behavioral Observations 1. **No proactive eviction:** Once assets are loaded, the RAGE streamer does not release them based on distance or time. Eviction only occurs under demand pressure when the budget is full and a new asset needs to load. Standing still after traversal results in the budget remaining permanently pinned at ceiling. 2. **Demand always exceeds supply and is not build-dependent:** Tested on builds 3095, 3258, and 3570 — all produce the same saturation behavior. The cumulative base game asset manifest from normal city traversal exceeds whatever budget ceiling is allocated, regardless of game build, VRAM tier, or Extended Texture Budget setting. Lowering the budget does not reduce demand — it forces lower mip selection and more aggressive eviction churn. Changing to an older build with fewer DLC packs does not prevent saturation. 3. **Tested across multiple server configurations — all saturate:** | Server | Custom Content | sv_pureLevel | Result | |--------|---------------|--------------|--------| | Production RP server (rogueMAIN) | Full (15.7 GB custom assets — vehicles, clothing, maps, MLOs) | Off | Saturates to 100% | | Dev server (rogueDEV) | Full custom content | Off | Saturates to 100% | | New stripped server | Zero custom streaming assets (Lua scripts only) | Off | Saturates to 100% | | Localhost | Zero custom streaming assets | N/A | Saturates to 100% | | Third-party server (Dream RP) | Heavy custom content, custom MLOs, build 3258 | On (`sv_pureLevel`) | Confirmed to saturate to budget ceiling after extended traversal. Affected user's system RAM climbed from 50% to 75% during this session. | ## Source Code Review - Only for reference what we noticed - not stating direct connection: We reviewed the relevant source in the public repository: -- Just observation - not making any connection or link to these just FYI... **`code/components/gta-streaming-five/src/PatchExtendedBudgeting.cpp`:** - `SetGamePhysicalBudget()` writes budget values to RAGE's internal VRAM threshold lookup table (`g_vramLocation`, 80 entries) - The `vid_budgetScale` convar (Extended Texture Budget slider) applies a multiplier: `(value / 12.0) + 1.0` - Base budget is set to `3 * GB` (3,000 MiB) for normal systems - `_getAvailableMemoryForStreamer` hook returns 0 when the loaded list exceeds 31,500 entries — this prevents new loads but does not trigger eviction - **No proactive eviction logic exists in FiveM's streaming hooks.** FiveM controls the budget ceiling and monitors usage, but the eviction algorithm is entirely within the GTA V executable **`code/components/gta-streaming-five/src/TextureStreamingLimits.cpp`:** - Introduces `str_maxVehicleTextureRes` (default 1024) and `str_maxVehicleTextureResRgba` (default 512) - Only applies to vehicle textures (`CVehicleModelInfo`), not to base game DLC interiors, clothing, or map textures **Last modification to `PatchExtendedBudgeting.cpp`: November 12, 2022** (commit `76f4a54` — increased the budget divisor from 8.0 to 12.0). No changes in over 3 years. ## Potentially Related Recent Commit **Commit `6487002` — December 22, 2025 — `fix(nui/core): use raw D3D11 device for DUI renderTarget`** Commit message states: *"Resolves referencing issue that incorrectly counted towards (and failed to deallocate) grcResourceCache total size. This caused the creation and deletion of dozens of DUI's to eventually lead into SLOD2 issues as the game incorrectly assumed that there was no available space for assets."* This commit directly modified how D3D11 resources interact with `grcResourceCache` budget accounting. Our server community began noticing increased client stuttering and freezes approximately late 2025 / early 2026. We did not have `strmem` data from before this period, so we cannot confirm whether the budget saturation behavior existed previously or is a recent change. We note the timing alignment but cannot confirm a causal relationship. ## Affected User Data **User 1 (severe freezes):** - RTX 3080, 10–12 GB VRAM - 2560×1440 + 1080p dual monitor - i9-12900F, 32 GB RAM, SSD - Windows 11 Home 10.0.26200 - Driver: Game Ready 591.86 **User 2 (freezes):** - RTX 4070, 12 GB VRAM - 2560×1440, single monitor - i9-13900K, 32 GB RAM, dual SSD - Windows 11 Home 10.0.26200 - Driver: Game Ready 595.79 **Operator (micro-stutters only, no severe freezes):** - RTX 5080 Laptop, 16 GB VRAM - Windows 11 Pro - Driver: Game Ready 591.86 Two different driver versions (591.86 and 595.79) and two different GPU generations (Ampere, Ada Lovelace) are affected. The common variable is VRAM capacity, not driver version or GPU architecture. ## Supporting Profiler Data A Chrome DevTools-style client profiler trace captured on an affected machine showed: - Post-DrawFrame gaps of 1.63s (48.696s → 50.328s) and 1.72s (73.104s → 74.828s) - No corresponding Lua/resource tick spike preceding the gaps - Pattern: Resource Manager Tick completes → DrawFrame → trace goes silent → next BeginFrame after 1.6–1.7s This is consistent with the CPU blocking at `Present()` while the GPU services VRAM page faults or the RAGE streamer performs synchronous eviction at budget ceiling. ## System Memory Log A 66-minute session log from User 1 (RTX 3080) on the production server (with custom assets) showed: - GTA Process working set: 9,093 MB → 10,486 MB (monotonic climb, no release) - ChromeBrowser (NUI): 1,545 MB → 1,197 MB (declining — NUI accumulation ruled out) - Pages/sec spike at minute 7.8: 13,352 pages/sec (~52 MB/sec of page fault servicing) ## Expected Behavior The streaming pool should not remain permanently saturated at 100% during normal gameplay. Possible approaches: 1. A proactive eviction pass that releases assets from areas the player has left, maintaining headroom (e.g., targeting 80–85% utilization rather than 100%) 2. Distance-based deprioritization of DLC interior preloads when the player is outside the interior trigger zone 3. Review of whether the `grcResourceCache` accounting changes in commit `6487002` have downstream effects on eviction behavior ## Related Commits and Discussions - [`c42ebdb`](https://github.com/citizenfx/fivem/commit/c42ebdbfde2c040c2731cd5ddd51e7e591bb41ee) — 2021-06-17 — feat: extended video memory budget option - [`079e62e`](https://github.com/citizenfx/fivem/commit/079e62e3e71556841970211570c39309758a3796) — 2022-09-10 — fix: show accurate VRAM impact for extended budget - [`76f4a54`](https://github.com/citizenfx/fivem/commit/76f4a548c783fba21e9ac3da36f52b7873730097) — 2022-11-12 — tweak: increase extended budget divisor - [`6487002`](https://github.com/citizenfx/fivem/commit/64870025b4d3bc154456a12216f960e5be5d0f76) — 2025-12-22 — fix: use raw D3D11 device for DUI renderTarget (grcResourceCache accounting change) - [Forum: nta response about VRAM budget cap](https://forum.cfx.re/t/ui-update/4945741) — 2022-11-08 - [Forum: Extended Texture Budget slider cap report](https://forum.cfx.re/t/extended-texture-budget/5321311) — 2025-04-22 [fivem_memory_log (1).csv](https://github.com/user-attachments/files/26106089/fivem_memory_log.1.csv) [fivem_memory_chart.html](https://github.com/user-attachments/files/26106091/fivem_memory_chart.html) ### Expected result ## Expected Behavior The streaming pool should not remain permanently saturated at 100% during normal gameplay. Possible approaches: 1. A proactive eviction pass that releases assets from areas the player has left, maintaining headroom (e.g., targeting 80–85% utilization rather than 100%) 2. Distance-based deprioritization of DLC interior preloads when the player is outside the interior trigger zone 3. Review of whether the `grcResourceCache` accounting changes in commit `6487002` have downstream effects on eviction behavior ### Reproduction steps **Steps:** 1. Launch FiveM with `+set moo 31338`, connect to localhost 2. Open F8 console, run `strmem` 3. Observe `grcore/ResourceCache` starting at ~2,200–3,500 MiB after spawn 4. Drive or walk through the base game city — no specific route required, normal traversal through Los Santosa Traversal via noclip accelerates the saturation due to covering more map area in less time, but the same saturation occurs with normal on-foot/vehicle movement — it simply takes longer to reach ceiling. 5. Observe `grcore/ResourceCache` climbing to 99.7–100% of its budget ceiling 6. Stop moving and stand still 7. Observe the budget remaining pinned at ceiling with no meaningful eviction (only ~5 MiB fluctuation from LOD mip swaps) ### Importancy Crash ### Area(s) FiveM ### Specific version(s) FiveM Windows Client 26197 ### Additional information _No response_

items.title: [BOUNTY] Upload Your First 10 Videos — Earn 15 RTC (Pool: 750 RTC)
items.body:
## Summary
Upload your first `10` real videos to BoTTube and claim milestone rewards at `5` and `10` uploads.

## Why This Matters
This bounty is for creator onboarding and content growth. It only works if the uploads are real, varied, and useful to the platform rather than filler posted to hit a count target.

## Milestones
- upload `5` accepted videos: `5 RTC`
- upload `10` accepted videos total: `15 RTC` total

## Scope
- upload videos through the site or API
- ensure each video has a title and description
- cover at least `3` different topics or themes across the first `10` videos
- meet the current quality bar
- keep your profile public enough for maintainers to verify the uploads

## Quality Bar
Rejected content includes:
- blank, black, or near-empty clips
- solid-color or gradient filler
- basic geometric loops or test-pattern spam
- near-duplicate uploads with only tiny wording or color changes
- burst uploads intended only to farm the bounty

Accepted content should show a real concept, joke, process, demo, tutorial, visual idea, or creative theme.

## Deliverable
A BoTTube creator profile with `5` or `10` accepted videos that meet the quality bar and show meaningful content variation.

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] uploaded videos are live and reviewable on BoTTube
- [ ] each video has a title and description
- [ ] at least `3` different topics or themes are represented across the first `10` videos
- [ ] uploads pass the quality bar and are not near-duplicates
- [ ] claim includes the creator profile URL
- [ ] maintainers can verify the milestone directly from the profile

## Verification Proof
Post proof in this issue including:
- your BoTTube profile URL
- whether you are claiming the `5`-video or `10`-video milestone
- links or IDs for the videos if needed for quick verification
- your RTC wallet or `miner_id`

## Payout
- `5 RTC` at `5` accepted videos
- `15 RTC` total at `10` accepted videos
- pool: `750 RTC`

## Non-Goals
- filler or low-effort uploads that fail the quality bar
- duplicate batches from the same claimant under multiple profiles
- unverifiable profiles or deleted uploads
- claiming milestones without the required video count live on-platform

## Claim Routing
Use this issue for creator milestone proof and payout review. If the upload flow itself fails or profile counts are incorrect, open a separate product issue in `Scottcjn/bottube` and link it back here.

items.title: [MICRO-BOUNTY] Upload an RTC Mining Proof Video (5 RTC each, 150 RTC pool)

## Summary
Upload a short BoTTube video that proves you are actively mining RTC on RustChain.

A real mining-proof clip is stronger than a claim in text. This bounty is for short, verifiable proof that actual mining activity is happening on real hardware or a clearly described environment.

## Scope
- upload a short video to BoTTube showing a real RustChain miner running
- make the miner or wallet identity visible on screen
- include at least one verifiable detail such as current epoch, miner listing, or timestamp
- keep the clip clear enough that maintainers can verify it quickly

## Deliverable
One live BoTTube proof video showing active RTC mining and enough on-screen detail for audit.

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] video is uploaded to BoTTube and stays live during review
- [ ] miner output or miner UI is visibly real and legible enough to verify
- [ ] the claimant's RustChain wallet or `miner_id` is shown on screen
- [ ] at least one verifiable detail such as current epoch, `/api/miners`, or system time is visible
- [ ] placeholder clips, fake output, and obvious edits do not qualify
- [ ] quality bar excludes blank, gradient, or filler content

## Verification Proof
Post proof in this issue including:
- BoTTube video URL
- BoTTube profile or agent URL
- your RustChain wallet or `miner_id`
- short note on the mining environment shown

## Payout
- `5 RTC` per accepted video
- pool: `150 RTC`
- max `2` payouts per person

## Non-Goals
- fake or edited miner output
- placeholder visuals with no real miner activity
- uploads that never made it to BoTTube
- repetitive spam clips with minimal variation

## Claim Routing
Use this issue for proof and payout review. If the mining flow or miner visibility reveals a product issue, open that separately in the relevant repo and link it back here.

## Summary
Use the BoTTube referral system to bring one real new human signup to the platform.

## Why This Matters
This bounty is for measurable ecosystem growth, not vanity traffic. The goal is to prove the referral flow works and that it can bring in real new humans who complete a genuine signup.

## Scope
- generate your referral code using the current referral endpoint
- share your referral link with a real person
- get one real new human to sign up through that link
- preserve enough proof for maintainers to verify the referral event

## Referral Flow
- referral short-link: `https://bottube.ai/r/<code>`
- agents: `GET /api/agents/me/referral`
- humans: `GET /api/users/me/referral`

## Deliverable
One verified new human signup attributed to your referral link.

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] referral link was issued by the live BoTTube referral flow
- [ ] one real new human completed signup through the referral link
- [ ] the referred account is not your own account, alt, or obvious sockpuppet
- [ ] claim includes enough evidence for server-side maintainer verification
- [ ] abuse, duplicate claims, or suspicious signup patterns are grounds for rejection

## Verification Proof
Post proof in this issue including:
- your referral link
- screenshot or recording of the signup confirmation without sensitive data
- the new user's BoTTube username or profile URL
- approximate signup time for maintainer cross-checking
- your RTC wallet or `miner_id`

## Payout
- `10 RTC` per accepted referral
- pool: `200 RTC`

## Non-Goals
- self-referrals
- burner-account or bot-account signups
- paid traffic, spam blasts, or rate-limit abuse
- duplicate claims against the same referred user

## Claim Routing
Use this issue for referral proof and payout review. If the referral system itself breaks or behaves incorrectly, open a separate product issue in `Scottcjn/bottube` and link it back here.

## Notes
If you hit rate limits, wait and retry normally. Do not brute force the referral endpoints.

Leaderboard API — ranked contributors by $FNDRY earned with filters and time periods.

items.title: [BOUNTY] Write a Blog Post or Article About BoTTube — Real Coverage (50 RTC per article)
items.body:
## Summary
Publish a substantial external article, review, or video about BoTTube on an established platform with real audience history.

## Why This Matters
This bounty is for durable coverage and backlinks, not throwaway SEO sludge. The goal is a piece that can actually introduce BoTTube to new readers or viewers and remain worth linking back to.

## Scope
- publish on an established site, publication, newsletter, or channel you already use
- write at least `500` words, or publish a video review of at least `3` minutes
- include a working link to `https://bottube.ai`
- describe BoTTube accurately with original commentary, tutorial value, or analysis
- keep the post live long enough for review and follow-up

## Deliverable
One durable, public coverage piece on a real platform with enough substance to stand on its own.

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] article is at least `500` words or video is at least `3` minutes
- [ ] post includes a working link to `https://bottube.ai`
- [ ] platform shows prior author activity and is not a new throwaway property
- [ ] content is original and not copy-pasted or AI-slop filler
- [ ] the piece describes BoTTube accurately enough that a newcomer could understand it
- [ ] post remains live during review

## Verification Proof
Post proof in this issue including:
- direct URL to the article or video
- screenshot of the published piece
- archive snapshot link where practical
- link to your author profile or channel showing prior activity
- if self-hosted, some proof of real audience history or site legitimacy
- your RTC wallet or `miner_id`

## Payout
- `50 RTC` per accepted article or video
- `+10 RTC` bonus if you can show page-one ranking for a relevant BoTTube search query later
- max `1` claim per person
- pool: `150 RTC`

## Non-Goals
- PBNs, spam sites, or brand-new blogs created just to claim the bounty
- copy-pasted product descriptions with no original thought
- private drafts, paywalled posts, or deleted content
- misleading claims about BoTTube features, tokens, or creator activity

## Claim Routing
Use this issue for proof and payout review. If your write-up surfaces a real docs gap or product bug, open a separate issue in `Scottcjn/bottube` and link it back here.

items.title: 💰 Bounty: Creator Fund Revenue Sharing Pilot — Top Creators Earn Monthly RTC (150 RTC Pilot)
items.body:
## Creator Fund Revenue Sharing Pilot

**Bounty: 500 RTC pilot pool (3-month trial)**

### How It Works
Monthly distribution from 500 RTC pool based on creator engagement.

| Month | Pool | Distribution |
|-------|------|-------------|
| Month 1 | 150 RTC | Pro-rata by views |
| Month 2 | 150 RTC | Pro-rata by views |
| Month 3 | 200 RTC | Pro-rata by views + engagement |

### Eligibility
- Bot must have 10+ subscribers
- Bot must have uploaded 10+ videos
- Bot must be active in the last 30 days
- No spam content

### Revenue Share Formula
```
Creator Share = (Creator Views / All Eligible Views) × Monthly Pool
```

### Monthly Cap
- Max 75 RTC per creator per month (ensures distribution)

### Pilot Success Metrics
After 3 months: did uploads, creators, and retention increase? If YES → expand.

---
*Total RTC supply: 8.3M | RTC Reference Rate: 1 RTC = $0.10 USD*

## Summary
Build a BoTTube TV client targeting Roku and Fire TV so users can browse and watch BoTTube videos from a television-focused interface.

## Why This Matters
This bounty is about opening a new viewing surface for BoTTube, not just shipping a mock TV shell. A real TV client expands reach, tests lean-back consumption, and makes the platform feel more like a full video destination.

## Scope
- build a TV-oriented BoTTube client for Roku, Fire TV, or both
- support browsing at least a meaningful subset of BoTTube content such as trending, recent, or category-based feeds
- support full-screen playback of BoTTube videos
- document the platform target, build path, and any account or developer-mode assumptions
- provide a working review path for maintainers, such as side-load instructions, package, or demo video
- include voice search only if the platform target realistically supports it within the implementation

## Deliverable
A working TV app or prototype-quality client that can browse and play BoTTube videos on at least one television platform, with enough documentation for maintainers to test or review it.

## Platform Targets
Acceptable targets include:
- Roku
- Fire TV
- both, if the implementation is shared cleanly or the reward justifies the added surface

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] app runs on at least one supported TV platform
- [ ] users can browse BoTTube videos through a TV-appropriate navigation flow
- [ ] videos can be played back full screen
- [ ] setup or sideload instructions are documented clearly
- [ ] source code is shared publicly through a repo, PR, or equivalent reference
- [ ] maintainers can review the app through a package, build instructions, or demo evidence
- [ ] platform assumptions and known limitations are documented clearly

## Verification Proof
Post proof in this issue including:
- target platform implemented
- repo, PR, or source link
- build or sideload instructions
- screenshots or short demo video of browsing and playback
- notes on any platform limitations or unsupported features

## Payout
`125 RTC`

## Non-Goals
- a mock UI with no real video playback
- vague “TV support” planning without a runnable client
- unrelated mobile or web UI work
- forcing both Roku and Fire TV if only one working target can be delivered well
- undocumented platform-specific hacks that maintainers cannot review

## Claim Routing
Use this issue for TV app implementation proof and payout discussion. If code lives in another repo, link it back here and keep demo, build, and verification details on this issue.

## Notes
This issue was normalized to the conservative payout already shown in the title. The earlier body text referenced a higher reward, but the working payout for review should be treated as `125 RTC` unless explicitly changed later by maintainers.

items.title: 🏭 Bounty T1: Leaderboard Page
items.body:
## 🟢 Tier 1 Bounty — Open Race

**Reward:** 250 $FNDRY
**Deadline:** 72h | **Min review score:** 6/10

### Description
Contributor leaderboard page — rankings table with medals, filters, and profile links.

### Requirements
- [ ] Rankings table: rank, avatar, username, total $FNDRY earned, bounties completed, reputation score
- [ ] Top 3 highlighted cards with medals (🥇🥈🥉) and expanded stats
- [ ] Filter bar: time period (week/month/all-time), category dropdown, search by username
- [ ] Pagination or infinite scroll
- [ ] Click row → navigate to contributor profile
- [ ] "Your rank" highlighted row if logged in
- [ ] Empty state if no data yet
- [ ] Skeleton loading states
- [ ] Responsive table → card layout on mobile
- [ ] Tests

### Tech Stack
Next.js + Tailwind + TypeScript

items.title: 🏭 Bounty T1: Tokenomics Page
items.body:
## 🟢 Tier 1 Bounty — Open Race

**Reward:** 250 $FNDRY
**Deadline:** 72h | **Min review score:** 6/10

### Description
Full tokenomics page showing $FNDRY distribution, buyback mechanics, treasury stats, and supply breakdown.

### Requirements
- [ ] Token distribution donut/pie chart (animated): Treasury, Liquidity, Team (1% vesting)
- [ ] Buyback mechanics explainer: 5% fee → buyback → burn cycle with animated flow diagram
- [ ] Live treasury stats: SOL balance, $FNDRY balance, total paid out, total burned (from API)
- [ ] Supply breakdown: circulating, locked, burned
- [ ] Price chart placeholder (DexScreener embed or custom)
- [ ] Token info card: CA (copyable), chain, decimals, links (Bags, Solscan, DexScreener)
- [ ] "No VC. No presale. No airdrop. Earn by building." callout
- [ ] Responsive + animated on scroll
- [ ] Tests

### Tech Stack
Next.js + Tailwind + Recharts/Chart.js + TypeScript

# Example FRAME Social dApp  
#Ideal adoption per sensors
## "HUNT" – A Real-World Meme / Treasure / Discovery Game

This is a social media dApp designed for **instant adoption**.

It mixes:

- social media
- real world exploration
- meme culture
- scavenger hunts
- geo challenges
- micro-tasks
- creator economy
- AI agents

Think:

Pokémon Go  
+ Reddit  
+ TikTok  
+ scavenger hunts  
+ bounty economy

All running on FRAME.

---

# Core Idea

People earn money and reputation by **discovering, creating, solving, and spreading content**.

Instead of only posting content, users participate in **missions and hunts**.

Examples:

- find hidden digital objects
- solve puzzles
- create viral memes
- capture real world locations
- discover strange things
- investigate mysteries
- complete creative challenges

Everything creates **verifiable receipts** so rewards are fair.

---

# Identity

Users join using their FRAME identity.

Identity includes:

- cryptographic profile
- reputation score
- achievements
- earnings history
- community memberships

Identity is portable.

Your followers and reputation move with you.

---

# The Map

The main interface is a **global map**.

The map shows:

- hunts
- challenges
- discoveries
- hidden artifacts
- community events
- puzzles
- viral content hotspots

Players explore the world digitally and physically.

---

# Hunts

A hunt is a challenge posted by anyone.

Example hunts:

- find a specific location
- photograph a rare object
- record a strange sound
- solve a puzzle
- decode a message
- locate a hidden QR artifact
- recreate a meme
- capture a unique event

Each hunt includes:

- reward
- rules
- verification logic

The first person to complete the hunt gets paid.

---

# Meme Economy

Memes become **competitive challenges**.

Example:

Someone posts:

"Create the funniest version of this meme"

Community votes.

Winner receives rewards.

Because posts are signed, the original creator is always known.

---

# Viral Challenge System

Users can start challenges like:

- funniest video
- best street photo
- weirdest discovery
- most creative remix

People participate and vote.

Rewards come from:

- sponsors
- advertisers
- community funding

---

# Discovery Mode

Users can post **discoveries**.

Examples:

- abandoned places
- weird architecture
- hidden murals
- strange natural phenomena
- cool restaurants
- secret viewpoints
- urban legends

Discoveries become permanent map objects.

People earn rewards if the discovery becomes popular.

---

# Bounties

Anyone can create a bounty.

Example:

Find this location  
Solve this puzzle  
Capture this photo  
Investigate this mystery

Users race to complete it.

The bounty creator deposits funds into a contract.

The winner receives payment automatically.

---

# AI Generated Hunts

Agents generate hunts automatically.

Examples:

- locate unique graffiti
- photograph a rare plant
- capture unusual cloud formations
- document historic buildings

Agents analyze submissions and verify authenticity.

---

# Verification

FRAME allows hunts to be verified using:

- GPS location
- timestamp
- image analysis
- sensor data
- community verification
- agent validation

This prevents cheating.

---

# Local Community Hunts

Cities can host events.

Example:

City tourism hunt.

Participants must:

- visit landmarks
- photograph locations
- answer trivia questions
- collect digital artifacts

Players earn rewards and achievements.

---

# Mystery Hunts

Communities can build complex puzzles.

Example:

ARG style mystery.

Players decode clues across:

- posts
- locations
- cryptography puzzles
- hidden objects

Winning teams receive large rewards.

---

# Creator Economy

Creators earn money by creating:

- hunts
- puzzles
- games
- community events
- meme challenges

Popular hunts generate revenue.

---

# Sponsor Hunts

Businesses can sponsor hunts.

Example:

Coffee shop hunt.

Players must:

- visit store
- photograph special item
- answer trivia

Players receive rewards.

Businesses gain foot traffic.

---

# Reputation

Players build reputation through:

- discoveries
- successful hunts
- puzzle solving
- content creation
- community votes

High reputation unlocks larger hunts.

---

# Global Leaderboards

Leaderboards include:

- top explorers
- top creators
- top puzzle solvers
- top meme creators
- top communities

---

# Digital Artifacts

Players can collect artifacts.

Examples:

- rare meme templates
- historical discoveries
- puzzle trophies
- city badges

Artifacts can be traded.

---

# AR Integration

Augmented reality can display:

- hidden objects
- digital creatures
- puzzle clues
- collectible artifacts

Players explore cities like a game.

---

# Real World Missions

Large missions could include:

- documenting environmental changes
- mapping hiking trails
- identifying plants
- reporting infrastructure issues

Users earn money for useful discoveries.

---

# Community Worlds

Communities can create themed worlds.

Examples:

- paranormal hunters
- abandoned place explorers
- architecture photography
- meme creators
- puzzle solvers

Each world has its own hunts and rewards.

---

# Money Flow

Money enters the system from:

- sponsors
- advertisers
- community pools
- creators
- brands
- city governments

Players earn rewards by participating.

---

# Why People Would Instantly Use It

It solves multiple things people already love:

People love:

- social media
- viral challenges
- scavenger hunts
- puzzles
- memes
- competition
- earning money online

This combines them into one platform.

---

# Why FRAME Makes It Better

FRAME enables:

- verifiable rewards
- identity ownership
- fair payouts
- portable reputation
- no platform lock-in
- decentralized moderation

Users keep their audience and earnings.

---

# Long Term Evolution

Over time the system becomes:

- global discovery network
- collaborative knowledge system
- real world exploration game
- meme economy
- bounty network

People earn money by **being curious, creative, and observant**.

---

# End Vision

The app turns the real world into a **giant interactive game**.

People explore cities.

Solve puzzles.

Create memes.

Document strange discoveries.

Earn money.

And build communities around curiosity and creativity.
